items.title: Always verify after squaring (extraneous-root audit)
items.description: Squaring both sides of an equation can introduce solutions that don’t satisfy the original, so candidates must be checked back in the unsquared equation.
items.content: After squaring, solve the resulting polynomial but then substitute each candidate into the original equation (or at least into the unsquared relation plus sign constraints). This prevents incorrect inclusion of extraneous roots that arise solely from the transformation.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts follow a common pattern: they reduce the equation to a simpler algebraic form by substituting a quadratic expression, then square only after establishing the domain/sign conditions, and finally verify each candidate solution against the original (unsquared) equation—rejecting extraneous roots created by squaring. Unsuccessful attempts typically fail in one of two ways: (1) they compute products using all algebraic candidates from the squared equation without checking which satisfy the original equation, or (2) they introduce sign/absolute-value handling mistakes (e.g., effectively changing the equation by taking |·| without maintaining consistency), or (3) they make an incorrect algebraic factorization/discriminant/derived quadratic leading to wrong candidate values.

items.title: Use sign/domain constraints to prune before committing
items.description: Before and after a risky transformation (like squaring), explicitly enforce the nonnegativity conditions required by radicals and by the left-hand side.
items.content: Radicals impose domain constraints (e.g., radicand ≥ 0) and the unsquared equality often requires the left side to be ≥ 0 as well. Apply these constraints early (before or immediately after solving the squared equation) to eliminate impossible branches quickly.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts follow a common pattern: they reduce the equation to a simpler algebraic form by substituting a quadratic expression, then square only after establishing the domain/sign conditions, and finally verify each candidate solution against the original (unsquared) equation—rejecting extraneous roots created by squaring. Unsuccessful attempts typically fail in one of two ways: (1) they compute products using all algebraic candidates from the squared equation without checking which satisfy the original equation, or (2) they introduce sign/absolute-value handling mistakes (e.g., effectively changing the equation by taking |·| without maintaining consistency), or (3) they make an incorrect algebraic factorization/discriminant/derived quadratic leading to wrong candidate values.

items.title: Choose a substitution that makes the equation one-variable after simplification
items.description: Introduce a carefully chosen auxiliary variable representing the repeated quadratic expression to reduce complexity and make factoring/discriminant steps clean.
items.content: When an equation contains the same quadratic form in both the polynomial side and inside a square root, set that form equal to a new variable. This turns a messy expression in x into a simpler equation in the new variable, which can often be solved by factoring.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts follow a common pattern: they reduce the equation to a simpler algebraic form by substituting a quadratic expression, then square only after establishing the domain/sign conditions, and finally verify each candidate solution against the original (unsquared) equation—rejecting extraneous roots created by squaring. Unsuccessful attempts typically fail in one of two ways: (1) they compute products using all algebraic candidates from the squared equation without checking which satisfy the original equation, or (2) they introduce sign/absolute-value handling mistakes (e.g., effectively changing the equation by taking |·| without maintaining consistency), or (3) they make an incorrect algebraic factorization/discriminant/derived quadratic leading to wrong candidate values.

items.title: Be rigorous with absolute values and sign splits
items.description: If you rewrite a square root in terms of an absolute value, you must maintain the correct sign conditions consistently across cases.
items.content: Transformations involving √(something like y²) require replacing with |y| and then checking sign cases carefully. Any shortcut that implicitly assumes a sign (or mishandles |·|) can yield incorrect solutions that still solve the transformed equation but not the original.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts follow a common pattern: they reduce the equation to a simpler algebraic form by substituting a quadratic expression, then square only after establishing the domain/sign conditions, and finally verify each candidate solution against the original (unsquared) equation—rejecting extraneous roots created by squaring. Unsuccessful attempts typically fail in one of two ways: (1) they compute products using all algebraic candidates from the squared equation without checking which satisfy the original equation, or (2) they introduce sign/absolute-value handling mistakes (e.g., effectively changing the equation by taking |·| without maintaining consistency), or (3) they make an incorrect algebraic factorization/discriminant/derived quadratic leading to wrong candidate values.

items.title: Double-check algebraic factorization and discriminant calculations
items.description: Small arithmetic or factoring errors can propagate into wrong candidate solutions, so verify key algebra steps like factoring and discriminants.
items.content: After expanding and collecting terms, confirm factoring or discriminant computations (e.g., by re-expanding the factored form). This reduces the risk of generating incorrect roots that then look “valid” under the wrong polynomial.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts follow a common pattern: they reduce the equation to a simpler algebraic form by substituting a quadratic expression, then square only after establishing the domain/sign conditions, and finally verify each candidate solution against the original (unsquared) equation—rejecting extraneous roots created by squaring. Unsuccessful attempts typically fail in one of two ways: (1) they compute products using all algebraic candidates from the squared equation without checking which satisfy the original equation, or (2) they introduce sign/absolute-value handling mistakes (e.g., effectively changing the equation by taking |·| without maintaining consistency), or (3) they make an incorrect algebraic factorization/discriminant/derived quadratic leading to wrong candidate values.

items.title: Constraint-first coordinate formulation
items.description: Turn the diagram into explicit equations by modeling the circle center as an unknown point and enforcing “on the circle” constraints.
items.content: Choose coordinates aligned to the given perpendicularity so that known points have simple coordinates. Then write one equation per known point stating the squared distance to the unknown center equals the radius squared. Solve the resulting system (often subtracting equations to eliminate quadratic terms) and compute the requested squared distance from the solved center.
items.reasoning:
Correct attempts mostly used a coordinate/constraint approach: explicitly represent the circle center as an unknown point, place the right angle with axes, write two “on the circle” distance equations from the center to two known points, subtract them to get a linear relation, then solve and compute the desired squared distance. They also handled the ambiguity of multiple solutions by checking which one matches the diagram’s placement (e.g., choosing the smaller/appropriate distance when the figure indicates the center is on a particular side).
Incorrect attempts tended to (1) assume a chord/segment relationship (or “power of a point” form) without verifying that the required configuration for that theorem holds (e.g., treating the right-angle point as if it were interior-on-the-chord when it isn’t, or confusing near vs. far intersection products), or (2) make an inconsistent coordinate placement that conflicts with the given geometry (leading to wrong radius/sign conclusions), or (3) use an unvalidated formula for the distance from center to chord and incorrectly equate it to the distance from the vertex to the center.
Overall, success came from translating the diagram into correct constraints and then solving systematically, while failure came from applying powerful theorems to the wrong geometry configuration or from making coordinate assumptions that don’t match the actual figure.

items.title: Validate theorem prerequisites (especially power/chord claims)
items.description: When using “power of a point” or chord-distance formulas, verify that the needed configuration (which intersections, near vs. far products, secant/tangent structure) actually matches the diagram.
items.content: Power-of-a-point identities depend on how the line from the external point meets the circle and which intersection distances are multiplied. If you use a simplified product like “AB·BC,” check that those are the correct near/far intersection lengths for the same line secant(s) required by the theorem. Otherwise, the computed OB² will be systematically wrong even if the algebra is fine.

items.title: Handle multiple geometric solutions by matching diagram placement
items.description: If your equations yield multiple possible center positions, determine which one is consistent with the figure before selecting the final value.
items.content: Solving distance-constraint systems often produces two symmetric solutions. Use qualitative information from the diagram (which side of the notch, relative vertical/horizontal placement, “center nearer to this region,” etc.) to pick the physically/graphically valid one, then compute the requested quantity for that choice.

items.title: Don’t confuse chord distance with point-to-center distance
items.description: Ensure the quantity you’re solving for matches the theorem’s output (distance to a chord vs. distance from a specific vertex to the center).
items.content: Formulas like the relationship between chord length and the perpendicular distance from the center apply to a specific “center-to-chord” distance, not automatically to distances from an arbitrary point on the chord line unless perpendicular projection and location are verified. When in doubt, revert to coordinates where the desired distance is directly computable.

items.title: Use Complement Events to Simplify “At Least” Conditions
items.description: When a direct adjacency condition is hard to count, switch to the complement event that forbids the adjacency pattern.
items.content: Rephrase the target probability (“at least one/at least two adjacent”) as 1 minus a simpler event (“no adjacent among the chosen”). This typically reduces the problem to counting subsets with a global restriction, which is often more systematic than counting the desired complex event directly.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a common, correct structure: they convert the “at least two adjacent” requirement into a cleaner complement (“no two adjacent”), then use a reliable counting formula for independent sets on a cycle (or an equivalent circular non-adjacent counting method). The unsuccessful attempt fails by mishandling the circular-to-linear “spacing” correspondence: it counts compositions of gaps as if they were in one-to-one correspondence with subsets, but on a circle this overcounts/undercounts unless a correct circular adjustment factor is included. Other correct solutions also implicitly succeed by consistently applying and simplifying the same validated count (including the standard factor that fixes circular adjacency counting).

items.title: Apply the Correct Circular Non-Adjacency Counting Formula
items.description: Counting “no two adjacent” subsets on a circle requires a circular-specific formula, not the same reasoning as a line.
items.content: Use the known count (or derive carefully) for choosing k non-adjacent vertices on an n-cycle; it includes a factor that accounts for rotational/circular constraints. If you instead use a linear gap-composition argument without adjusting for circular overcounting, you can get inconsistent non-integer or wrong totals.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a common, correct structure: they convert the “at least two adjacent” requirement into a cleaner complement (“no two adjacent”), then use a reliable counting formula for independent sets on a cycle (or an equivalent circular non-adjacent counting method). The unsuccessful attempt fails by mishandling the circular-to-linear “spacing” correspondence: it counts compositions of gaps as if they were in one-to-one correspondence with subsets, but on a circle this overcounts/undercounts unless a correct circular adjustment factor is included. Other correct solutions also implicitly succeed by consistently applying and simplifying the same validated count (including the standard factor that fixes circular adjacency counting).

items.title: Validate Gap/Spacing Models Against One-to-One Correspondence
items.description: Any gap-based model must be checked for an exact bijection between counted structures and the original combinatorial objects.
items.content: When modeling circle selections via “gaps” around the circle, verify whether each subset corresponds to exactly one gap pattern (and vice versa). If the mapping depends on a starting point or rotation, you must include the correct normalization factor; otherwise the count will be systematically off.

items.title: Constraint-First via Prime Valuation
items.description: Reformulate “prime divides a combinatorial integer” into an explicit exponent/valuation condition before searching.
items.content: When asked for prime factors of expressions like binomial coefficients, convert divisibility into a statement about the exponent of a prime in the expression. Use a reliable theorem (e.g., Legendre’s formula for factorial valuations or Kummer/Lucas for binomial coefficients) to obtain a criterion that can be checked efficiently and that prevents incorrect global claims.
items.reasoning:
The successful attempts share a consistent, correct use of prime divisibility/valuation: they translate the question into an exponent-of-prime condition and then exploit a strong theorem (Legendre/Lucas/Kummer) to reduce the work to a small, structured set of checks—typically only primes up to a certain bound (e.g., because for large primes only the first term matters, or because carries/valuation vanish). They also systematically test candidates in descending order and justify why no larger candidate can work.

The incorrect attempts fail in a few characteristic ways: (1) they overgeneralize about primes larger than one of the factorial parameters, incorrectly concluding that all primes in a wide interval must divide the binomial coefficient; (2) they make a flawed “threshold” argument about when the valuation must be zero, eliminating primes that actually divide; and (3) they sometimes do the right kind of computation but with an incorrect bounding logic, leading to an incorrect cutoff (e.g., thinking the answer must be below a certain prime when the true transition happens elsewhere). Overall, the distinguishing factor is whether the argument includes a correct invariant/criterion (valuation via Legendre or carry via Kummer) and a correctly justified bound for which primes must be checked.

items.title: Use the Right Theorem to Shrink the Search Space
items.description: Choose a characterization (valuation or carry count) that makes “largest factor” reduce to checking only few candidates.
items.content: Successful solutions exploit structure: for sufficiently large primes, higher powers contribute nothing (so only the first term matters), or Kummer’s theorem reduces divisibility to whether a carry occurs. This enables a proof that primes above a certain range cannot work, allowing a descending candidate test without brute force.
items.reasoning:
The successful attempts share a consistent, correct use of prime divisibility/valuation: they translate the question into an exponent-of-prime condition and then exploit a strong theorem (Legendre/Lucas/Kummer) to reduce the work to a small, structured set of checks—typically only primes up to a certain bound (e.g., because for large primes only the first term matters, or because carries/valuation vanish). They also systematically test candidates in descending order and justify why no larger candidate can work.

The incorrect attempts fail in a few characteristic ways: (1) they overgeneralize about primes larger than one of the factorial parameters, incorrectly concluding that all primes in a wide interval must divide the binomial coefficient; (2) they make a flawed “threshold” argument about when the valuation must be zero, eliminating primes that actually divide; and (3) they sometimes do the right kind of computation but with an incorrect bounding logic, leading to an incorrect cutoff (e.g., thinking the answer must be below a certain prime when the true transition happens elsewhere). Overall, the distinguishing factor is whether the argument includes a correct invariant/criterion (valuation via Legendre or carry via Kummer) and a correctly justified bound for which primes must be checked.

items.title: Justify Thresholds Carefully (Don’t Guess the Cutoff)
items.description: Any argument that rules out whole ranges of primes must be rigorously tied to the exact valuation/carry criterion.
items.content: Incorrect attempts often came from incorrect “if p is in this interval then it must not divide” reasoning. To avoid this, derive the cutoff directly from the valuation formula or carry condition (e.g., comparing floor terms or the carry inequality), then verify it applies to all primes in that range.

items.title: Descending Candidate Testing with a Completeness Proof
items.description: When searching for the largest prime factor, test candidates in descending order but always include why you’ve exhausted all larger possibilities.
items.content: A robust approach checks primes from largest downward and stops at the first that satisfies the criterion only after proving every larger prime fails by the derived bound. This turns “we tried a few numbers” into a complete argument.

Overall, the distinguishing factor is whether the argument includes a correct invariant/criterion (valuation via Legendre or carry via Kummer) and a correctly justified bound for which primes must be checked.

items.title: Validate “Large Prime” Intuitions Against the Denominator
items.description: Be cautious with arguments that treat large primes as if they automatically appear in the numerator but never cancel with the denominator.
items.content: Some wrong solutions incorrectly assumed primes above a parameter must divide the whole binomial coefficient. The correct method must account for cancellation by the denominator via the exponent/valuation difference; large primes can still fail if the numerator’s exponent equals or is outweighed by the denominator’s exponent.

They also systematically test candidates in descending order and justify why no larger candidate can work.

The incorrect attempts fail in a few characteristic ways: (1) they overgeneralize about primes larger than one of the factorial parameters, incorrectly concluding that all primes in a wide interval must divide the binomial coefficient; (2) they make a flawed “threshold” argument about when the valuation must be zero, eliminating primes that actually divide; and (3) they sometimes do the right kind of computation but with an incorrect bounding logic, leading to an incorrect cutoff (e.g., thinking the answer must be below a certain prime when the true transition happens elsewhere). Overall, the distinguishing factor is whether the argument includes a correct invariant/criterion (valuation via Legendre or carry via Kummer) and a correctly justified bound for which primes must be checked.
</reasoning>

<memories>
<memory>
<title>Constraint-First via Prime Valuation</title>
<description>Reformulate “prime divides a combinatorial integer” into an explicit exponent/valuation condition before searching.</description>
<content>When asked for prime factors of expressions like binomial coefficients, convert divisibility into a statement about the exponent of a prime in the expression. Use a reliable theorem (e.g., Legendre’s formula for factorial valuations or Kummer/Lucas for binomial coefficients) to obtain a criterion that can be checked efficiently and that prevents incorrect global claims.</content>
</memory>

<memory>
<title>Use the Right Theorem to Shrink the Search Space</title>
<description>Choose a characterization (valuation or carry count) that makes “largest factor” reduce to checking only few candidates.</description>
<content>Successful solutions exploit structure: for sufficiently large primes, higher powers contribute nothing (so only the first term matters), or Kummer’s theorem reduces divisibility to whether a carry occurs. This enables a proof that primes above a certain range cannot work, allowing a descending candidate test without brute force.</content>
</memory>

<memory>
<title>Justify Thresholds Carefully (Don’t Guess the Cutoff)</title>
<description>Any argument that rules out whole ranges of primes must be rigorously tied to the exact valuation/carry criterion.</description>
<content>Incorrect attempts often came from incorrect “if p is in this interval then it must not divide” reasoning. To avoid this, derive the cutoff directly from the valuation formula or carry condition (e.g., comparing floor terms or the carry inequality), then verify it applies to all primes in that range.</content>
</memory>

<memory>
<title>Descending Candidate Testing with a Completeness Proof</title>
<description>When searching for the largest prime factor, test candidates in descending order but always include why you’ve exhausted all larger possibilities.</description>
<content>A robust approach checks primes from largest downward and stops at the first that satisfies the criterion only after proving every larger prime fails by the derived bound. This turns “we tried a few numbers” into a complete argument.</content>
</memory>

<memory>
<title>Validate “Large Prime” Intuitions Against the Denominator</title>
<description>Be cautious with arguments that treat large primes as if they automatically appear in the numerator but never cancel with the denominator.</description>
<content>Some wrong solutions incorrectly assumed primes above a parameter must divide the whole binomial coefficient. The correct method must account for cancellation by the denominator via the exponent/valuation difference; large primes can still fail if the numerator’s exponent equals or is outweighed by the denominator’s exponent.</content>
</memory>
</memories>

items.title: Constraint-First Multiset Formalization
items.description: Reformulate the problem’s “exactly two identical digits” requirement as a precise digit-multiplicity pattern before counting.
items.content: Translate the qualitative statement into an explicit multiset form (e.g., one digit occurs exactly twice and the other two digits occur exactly once, with the repeated digit distinct from the singletons). This prevents ambiguous interpretations like “at least” or “includes repeats” and gives a clear checklist of what must be disallowed.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts converged on a consistent meta-strategy: explicitly formalize the digit-multiset condition (“exactly one digit repeats exactly twice; the others are distinct singletons”) and then perform clean casework based on what the repeated digit is, being careful to enforce that no third copy of that digit or second repeated digit is introduced. The unsuccessful attempts typically failed by miscounting remaining-digit choices (e.g., allowing forbidden digits that would create extra repeats, or treating “choices” as if they were independent when they weren’t), or by under-specifying the “exactly two identical digits” constraint, leading to overcount or missing restrictions.

items.title: Casework on the Repeated Digit, Not on Positions
items.description: Choose cases based on what digit is repeated (e.g., whether it equals a fixed leading digit) and then count placements within each case.
items.content: Split into disjoint cases where the identity of the repeated digit is determined, because that automatically controls which digits are forbidden in the remaining slots. Then count placements and remaining digits using combinations/arrangements that match the fixed multiplicity structure.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts converged on a consistent meta-strategy: explicitly formalize the digit-multiset condition (“exactly one digit repeats exactly twice; the others are distinct singletons”) and then perform clean casework based on what the repeated digit is, being careful to enforce that no third copy of that digit or second repeated digit is introduced. The unsuccessful attempts typically failed by miscounting remaining-digit choices (e.g., allowing forbidden digits that would create extra repeats, or treating “choices” as if they were independent when they weren’t), or by under-specifying the “exactly two identical digits” constraint, leading to overcount or missing restrictions.

items.title: Enforce “No Additional Repeats” in Remaining Choices
items.description: Whenever you pick remaining digits after placing a repeated pair, explicitly exclude digits that would create a second repeated digit or a third occurrence.
items.content: After selecting the repeated digit, the remaining digits must be chosen under “distinctness” constraints: they cannot equal the repeated digit and cannot equal each other. A common counting error is to count them without fully applying these exclusions, yielding overcounts.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts converged on a consistent meta-strategy: explicitly formalize the digit-multiset condition (“exactly one digit repeats exactly twice; the others are distinct singletons”) and then perform clean casework based on what the repeated digit is, being careful to enforce that no third copy of that digit or second repeated digit is introduced. The unsuccessful attempts typically failed by miscounting remaining-digit choices (e.g., allowing forbidden digits that would create extra repeats, or treating “choices” as if they were independent when they weren’t), or by under-specifying the “exactly two identical digits” constraint, leading to overcount or missing restrictions.

items.title: Disjoint Cases and Independence Checks
items.description: Ensure cases are disjoint and that each counting factor is conditioned on earlier choices.
items.content: Before multiplying factors, verify that the choice counts truly refer to the remaining allowable set given prior selections (e.g., excluding already-used digits). Many wrong solutions fail because they treat a number of options as constant even after constraints reduce it.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts converged on a consistent meta-strategy: explicitly formalize the digit-multiset condition (“exactly one digit repeats exactly twice; the others are distinct singletons”) and then perform clean casework based on what the repeated digit is, being careful to enforce that no third copy of that digit or second repeated digit is introduced. The unsuccessful attempts typically failed by miscounting remaining-digit choices (e.g., allowing forbidden digits that would create extra repeats, or treating “choices” as if they were independent when they weren’t), or by under-specifying the “exactly two identical digits” constraint, leading to overcount or missing restrictions.
